entry.title: What exactly is SAP BW PCE… and what it is not !
entry.content:
Dear BW enthusiasts!
In February this year, SAP announced SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC). Amongst others, it combines the strengths of SAP Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud and provides many innovations including a deep integration with SAP Databricks for ML and AI use cases. In addition, the term “BW Modernization” also plays a key role in this new environment.

This blog post is meant to clarify the product behind this term, which is sometimes referred to SAP BW[4] PCE. Other terms like RISE-BW, BW cloud private edition, BW tailored option, BW BDC, SAP BW NetWeaver Cloud private edition, or SAP Business Warehouse Cloud are sometimes used in this context as well and that altogether leads to a high level of confusion. So, if you feel the same – keep on reading…

So let’s start to define the term exactly: What is this SAP BW[4] PCE ?
PCE means private cloud edition and refers to a deployment in the SAP private cloud as opposed to on-premise deployments or deployments at hosting/service partners.
BW[4] is an artificial umbrella term we at SAP like to use referring to both products equally:  SAP NetWeaver BW 7.5 on HANA and SAP BW/4HANA. You surely know that both products are quite different; but here the focus is not on features&functions, but on the private cloud deployment mode.
So, the image above means, that you are on SAP´s recommended BW modernization path if you run SAP BW 7.5 on HANA or SAP BW/4HANA in SAP´s private cloud.

What is SAP BW[4] PTO?
PTO stands for private cloud tailored option and refers to infrastructure configuration in the private cloud deployment, e.g. CPU power, memory size, disc space, disaster recovery&backup concepts, SLAs just name some typical ones.
In standard PCE terms, this configuration is defined by the well-known T-shirt size approach concept. But if customer requirements cannot be covered there, a more specific approach can be designed in a “tailored option”. So in a nutshell, PTO is a dedicated flavor of PCE to meet individual customer needs regarding infrastructure.
In this blog and many other sources, the term PCE includes PTO flavors as we focus on the deployment in the private cloud only irrespective of infrastructure details. So if you read SAP BW PCE, a the system might run also with the tailored option.

What is SAP BW[4] PCE not?
Very clearly – SAP BW[4] PCE is not a new or revised product. It is not a new codeline.
It is the same codeline as BW on HANA or BW/4HANA on premise.
Also worth mentioning - it is not a public cloud solution.
Finally, it does not restrict any existing features. In terms of functionality SAP BW 7.5 on HANA  PCE provides 100% of the features of SAP BW 7.5 on HANA. And SAP BW/4HANA covers the exact same scope as SAP BW/4HANA PCE of the same release and SP level. 
Exception: The so-called Data Product Generator is available in PCE depoyments in BDC only – but what you currently use in SAP BW[4] on-premise is also available in BW[4] PCE. 

How to get to SAP BW[4] PCE?
If your BW system is managed by SAP already, then you are there already: You leverage BW[4] PCE.
For all other customers: a technical migration is needed from the current data center to a Hyperscaler data center managed by SAP. This migration is sometimes referred to as “Lift&Shift”, because in most cases it is a 1:1 move without any changes to the database or the ABAP application stack. It comes with a downtime for each system tier and some testing effort in similar scale as a SP upgrade of course, but expectation is generally that everything keeps working in PCE in a non-disruptive way.
If the system is currently running on a 3rd party database, a migration to SAP HANA is required, because PCE´s minimal requirements are BW 7.5 on HANA. You can also include a ABAP stack update or upgrade when moving to PCE.

SAP provides the RISE methodology including tools, concepts, processes, experts etc. to bring customer SAP-landscape into PCE. So, if customers sometimes refer to their “SAP RISE BW”, but actually mean BW[4] deployed in PCE after a successful RISE project delivered by SAP.

What is different with SAP BW[4] PCE in SAP Business Data Cloud?
If your SAP BW[4] PCE subscription includes SAP BDC capacity units, you have currently one additional feature: The Data Product Generator which derives data products from SAP BW[4] PCE InfoProviders into the HANA object store of SAP Datasphere. Based on this integration, customers can redesign relevant applications in SAP Datasphere + SAP Analytics Cloud step-by-step. SAP note 3590400 explains how to enable this additional function and SAP note 3584640 explains, that embedded BW scenarios are out of scope in general. Also refer to SAP BW/4HANA help portal.

What about system maintenance end dates?
One important value added of running BW 7.5 in PCE is the automatic maintenance extension of three years until end of 2030 with no additional fees. See SAP note 3590297 and the SAP Product Availability Matrix (PAM) for more details.

Summary
The purpose of this blog post is to explain the significance of a private cloud deployment for SAP BW or SAP BW/4HANA. We at SAP see this as the first crucial step towards a viable modernization shifting from classic BW data flows and BW queries towards Datasphere views and SAC reporting&planning. There are still open questions regarding the detailed modernization approach, and future publication will surely follow...
However, it should be clear, that the move into PCE based on a RISE migration project is a step into the right direction which finally ends in SAP Business Data Cloud including BW[4] PCE. In a nutshell:

  1. Go through a RISE project to bring your BW landscape to an SAP PCE deployment.
  2. Then, enhance your PCE subscription commercially to SAP Business Data Cloud and expand/shift BW scenarios to SAP Datasphere + SAP Analytics Cloud step by step.
  3. Finally adopt SAP BDC completely and decommission SAP BW[4] PCE.

Let´s also emphasize once more, that we are talking of one technical migration only. Shifting from PCE to BDC is mainly a commercial adjustment but does not result in a costly or complex technical migration.

Some useful notes

  • 0052505 - Support after end of mainstream maintenance or extended maintenance
  • 1648480 - Maintenance for SAP Business Suite 7 including SAP NetWeaver
  • 2741041 - Maintenance for SAP NetWeaver 7.x Business Warehouse
  • 2934895 - Maintenance for SAP BW/4HANA
  • 3590297 - Information about maintenance availability in SAP BW private cloud editions
  • 3590400 - Configuration SAP BW[4]: Data Product Generator for SAP Business Data Cloud
  • 3584640 - Data Product Generator in embedded SAP BW in SAP ERP and S/4HANA
  • SAP BW/4HANA help > Integrating Data from the Data Product Generator for SAP Business Data Cloud

Thanks to Alex for sharing his thoughts...

entry.title: Re: node rfc server issue being unreliable
entry.content:
Strange, looks fine so far.

In this case, there must be something very wrong either in the Python wrapper around libsapnwrfc.so, or in your Python code itself. Try setting the environment variable RFC_TRACE=4, before starting the server program. Then we can see, what the program is doing.

I have never used Python, but when doing it in C, I would do something like

RFC_ERROR_INFO errorInfo;
// Either manually created or obtained from ABAP DDIC:
RFC_FUNCTION_DESC_HANDLE myFuncDesc = ...;

RfcInstallServerFunction(cU("NPL"), myFuncDesc, handlerFunction, &errorInfo);

at the very beginning, and the function should be known for the runtime of the process. The only way to make it "unknown" again, is to call RfcInstallServerFunction a second time like this

RfcInstallServerFunction(cU("NPL"), myFuncDesc, NULL, &errorInfo);

If this is happening, we should see it in the RFC Trace.

entry.title: Re: Display Original Duplicate Triplicate on Delivery cha...
entry.content:
Dear @Sarat.

Yes, you can show “Original”, “Duplicate”, “Triplicate”, and “Extra Copy” on each copy of your Adobe Form in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

 Two common methods:

  1. Dynamic text using page number:
    • Add a text field in the master page header.
  1. Multiple master pages:
    • Create 4 master pages, each with its label.
    • Assign each page to a copy via pagination settings.

 Important:

  • This logic works only if all four copies are generated in one output run.
  • No ABAP logic allowed — all must be handled within the Adobe LiveCycle Designer form.

 

Adobe LiveCycle Designer Scripting Guide (JavaScript)
Download PDF

SAP Help – Forms by Adobe in S/4HANA Cloud

SAP Note 2834407 – Basics on designing Adobe Forms in S/4HANA Cloud

Best regards

Chuma

entry.title: Re: Knowledge sharing - SAP Multi-Client setup and experi...
entry.content:
Hi Michael, 

I would say, that the answer is spaces and DACs, if you mean MANDT (Mandant). If not, please ignore my response :). 

Multi-Client is from DB perspective just a table field. So you can load (persist) all the data into one inbound space and share via views to different spaces representing the clients. Also share all the data into the 'all-clients' space. 

Also possible to use one space and use DACs to control who can see what. I find this approach however problematic due to

#additional overhead in DACs

#space can be downed if no safety-belt is used. with multiple spaces one can control overload better. 

 

I come from BW, so I always try to build DSP as BW 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-datasphere-architecture-and-security-concept/ba-p/13702030

 

Cheers, 

Martin

 

May the SAP force be with you as well 🙂

entry.title: Advanced Lanes and Rate Management based on S/4Hana Transportation Management
entry.content:
Business Need

Transportation lanes are a foundational component in SAP Transportation Management (SAP TM), acting as a key master data object that defines the connection between source and destination locations along with associated planning attributes such as duration, cost, and carrier allocation. While the standard transportation lane setup in SAP TM effectively supports many planning scenarios, some customers require additional flexibility and automation in how these lanes are created and maintained.

In such scenarios, manual setup of transportation lanes can become time-consuming, particularly when dealing with high volumes or frequent changes. Attributes like business share or contracted durations often need to be updated in mass, which may not be fully addressed through standard mass maintenance tools.

Another area where a custom tailored solution can add value is in carrier selection and planning optimization. The standard optimizer typically uses static cost figures maintained in the transportation lane. While this approach works well in many use cases, some organizations may benefit from a more dynamic cost comparison mechanism—such as leveraging contracted carrier rates directly during optimization. This is particularly relevant in decision-making scenarios like Full Truck Load (FTL) vs. Less than Truck Load (LTL), where real-time or rate-based comparison can help drive more cost-effective and operationally aligned outcomes.

 

Advanced Lane and Rate Management Solution

To address the need for more dynamic and scalable transportation lane and rate management, we developed an custom tailored ready to use solution comprising two key components:

Lane Management Solution

Rate Management Solution

These two components work together to streamline lane creation, carrier selection, and rate maintenance based on real-time data and valid contractual agreements.

🔹Note: The Lane Management Solution requires the Rate Management Solution as a prerequisite, since lanes are derived from rate data. However, the Rate Management Solution can be deployed independently to simplify and centralize rate maintenance across multiple carriers.

Lane Management Solution

This solution enables the dynamic generation of transportation lanes by leveraging master rate data—agreements, calculation sheets and rates for the carriers—maintained in the system. 

Key Features:

  • Dynamic Lane Generation:
    Valid lanes are created in real time based on rates and their associated attributes, allowing for flexible and adaptive planning.

  • Optimizer Enhancement:
    A pre-delivered enhancement modifies the optimizer input by injecting dynamically derived lanes based on valid contracted rates instead of relying on pre-configured lanes.

  • Attribute-Based Carrier Selection:
    Carrier determination can now consider various freight order attributes such as weight, volume, equipment type, or port location. The solution also respects rate validity periods, excluding expired contracts automatically.

  • Enhanced Carrier Selection Strategy:
    A custom strategy enables planners to select carriers based on up-to-date rate data rather than fixed lane configurations.

  • Profile-Based Configuration:
    Planning and carrier selection profiles include new toggles that allow users to switch between the standard SAP behavior and the enhanced lane management logic.

  • Clean Core Compliant:
    Built in alignment with SAP’s clean core principles, ensuring upgradability and long-term sustainability.

Process Flow - Lane Management Solution

In the standard process flow, carrier selection and optimizer-based planning rely on pre-defined transportation lanes. The Lane Management Solution complements this approach by dynamically generating lanes from the rates master dataand supplying them to the optimizer, enabling more flexible planning and carrier selection.

Rate Management Solution

This component provides a centralized and user-friendly framework to manage rate master data, which acts as the backbone for both standalone rate usage and dynamic lane generation. Technically, the solution also delivers fast and robust queries on contracted rates, which serve as the foundation for building additional use cases—such as rate-based planning and carrier selection—further extending the value of rate master data within the planning process

Key Features:

  • User-Centric Dashboard:
    A POWL-based worklists (Personal Object Worklist) makes rate entry and maintenance easy and accessible.

  • Predefined Templates:
    Simplify rate creation by using standard templates tailored for various carrier types or scenarios.

  • Mass Upload/Download Utility:
    To streamline rate maintenance, users can leverage a user-friendly interface that supports spreadsheet uploads and downloads. This capability makes it easy to manage large volumes of rate data efficiently. One of the key advantages is the ability to update multiple rate tables in a single operation—saving time and effort compared to the standard process of uploading each table individually.

Together, the Lane and Rate Management Solutions provide a scalable, dynamic, and maintainable approach to transportation planning in SAP TM—enhancing the optimizer’s capability while reducing manual effort and increasing planning accuracy.

Solution Delivery:

This custom tailored solution has been maintained and is delivered by the SAP MaxAttention team as part of SAP Premium Engagement innovation initiative.

To learn more about how SAP Premium Engagement can help you accelerate your digital transformation, discover how our global team of experts supports customers in getting the most value from their SAP landscape.

If you’re already a Premium Engagement customer, reach out to your SAP Technical Quality Manager (TQM) to explore how our customized services can support your business needs and ensure the success of your SAP projects.

entry.title: Re: How to setup Time Evaluation in Time Tracking
entry.content:
Hi Timo,

I am currently stuck and need advice. The case is quite complicated, and I will explain it as best as possible. 

I have a client who has a particular overtime calculation. Most probably, some other experts have encountered it. The system has to evaluate all the working times from Monday to Sunday, and only what exceeds 42 hours per week should count as overtime. 

If I use the Time Recording Profile with Legacy Week, everything is super easy to configure I just use in the Time Valuations Valuation Method Valuate Whole Sheet or Period.

The challenge is setting this weekly overtime evaluation when using a Time Recording Approval Period, Length Month.

I am unable to find a suitable solution, and if you have any suggestions, I am all ears.

I want to mention that I tried several valuation scenarios, including using Time Container Types with Period Results – Week, which seemed to be the best course of action, but even with this, I end up with the following error: “You are not allowed to use more than 5 flexible period definitions. You are currently using 6 flexible period definitions.”

Any advice is greatly appreciated.

Thanks,

entry.title: Re: How to use Characteristic ID and Phrase Key to find l...
entry.content:
Hi Satya, 

From technical side, you could use either the BAPI or the Table mapping. 

If you just need the list as on output, I would personally prefer using the table mapping. 

The tables you listed are correct. Should give you the output. 

  •  ESTVA , ESTVP , ESTPP` for phrase/characteristic values
  •  ESTMJ` to link SUBID with MATNR

SUBID corresponds to RECN and then you can do all the mapping across above tables to get your output. 

From a functional side, you have various options available:

1. Use "Advanced Search" app

2. Use "Substance Workbench" (CG02) 

 

Hope this helps. Good luck.

 

Regards

Nischay

entry.title: 🚀 SAP Beginners: This isn't just about modules. It's about data's circulatory system
entry.content:
## SAP IDocs: The Essential Skill That Will Transform Your Career (A Beginner's Guide)

You're taking an exciting step into the world of SAP! As you navigate the various modules like Finance (FI), Materials Management (MM), Sales and Distribution (SD), or Production Planning (PP), you're building a strong foundation. But to truly stand out and unlock a deeper understanding of how businesses operate with SAP, you need to grasp the invisible threads that hold it all together: SAP IDocs.

Forget the buzzwords for a second. IDocs (Intermediate Documents) are the backbone of data exchange within the SAP ecosystem. They represent the standardized language that allows different SAP systems to communicate flawlessly with each other, and crucially, with external non-SAP systems and business partners. This isn't just theoretical; this is how real-world supply chains, complex financial operations, and intricate logistics processes actually work.

---

### What Exactly ARE IDocs? A Simple Analogy

At its core, an IDoc is a standardized data container. Think of it like a highly structured, electronic postcard or a digital manifest designed specifically for business transactions. Each IDoc carries specific business data (e.g., a customer order, a material master record, a payment advice) in a predefined format that both the sending and receiving systems can understand.

They are primarily used for asynchronous communication, meaning data is sent and received in separate steps, often in batches. This makes them incredibly robust and reliable for high-volume data exchange, even across different networks or systems that might not always be online simultaneously.

---

### Why Mastering IDocs is Non-Negotiable for Your SAP Career

Understanding IDocs isn't just about learning another transaction code; it's about gaining a holistic view of enterprise processes. This knowledge will set you apart and accelerate your career:

1. The "Why" Behind the "What": A Deeper Business Understanding
You know what a sales order is, but do you know how that order data travels from a customer's system to your SAP system, triggers production, and then generates an outbound delivery notification back to the customer? IDocs are the vehicle for this journey. By understanding them, you'll shift from merely knowing what an order is to understanding how that critical business data flows and integrates across an entire enterprise and beyond. This perspective is invaluable for designing robust solutions.

2. Your Troubleshooting Superpower: Solving Real-World Problems
Data integrations are complex, and things will go wrong. When a sales order isn't created in SAP despite the customer sending it, or a material master update fails to reach a subsidiary, where do you look? IDocs are your first line of defense. Knowing how to monitor, analyze, and reprocess stuck IDocs makes you an "IDoc detective" – an indispensable resource who can quickly diagnose and fix critical business process interruptions. This ability alone can save companies significant time and money.

3. Cross-Functional Versatility: Bridging Modules and Industries
IDocs aren't confined to a single SAP module. They facilitate data exchange for virtually every business area:
Logistics: Material Master (MATMAS), Sales Order (ORDERS), Purchase Order (ORDERS), Delivery (DESADV), Invoice (INVOIC).
Finance: Payment Advice (REMADV), Bank Statement (FINSTA).
HR: Employee Master (HRMD_A).
And many more!

By understanding IDocs, you gain a powerful tool for analyzing and working across different functional modules and even diverse industries. This broad applicability enhances your value as an SAP professional.

---

### Your First Steps: Essential IDoc Transaction Codes

Ready to get hands-on? Here are some fundamental transaction codes (T-codes) that will be your entry points into the world of IDocs. Familiarize yourself with their purpose and start exploring:

1. WE20: Partner Profiles - The Communication Address Book
Before any IDoc can be sent or received, SAP needs to know who it's talking to and how. WE20 (Partner Profiles) is where you define your communication partners (e.g., a specific customer, a vendor, another SAP system client). For each partner, you specify which Message Types (e.g., ORDERS, MATMAS) they will send or receive, and the technical details of that exchange (like the Port and Process Code). This is the absolute foundation for any IDoc communication.

2. WE81: Message Types - The Business Content
A Message Type defines the business meaning of the data being exchanged (e.g., "Purchase Order," "Material Master," "Invoice"). You'll refer to these constantly when discussing what kind of data an IDoc is carrying.

3. WE30: IDoc Type Development - The Technical Blueprint
While the Message Type tells you what the IDoc is about, the IDoc Type (e.g., ORDERS05, MATMAS06) defines its technical structure. It's the blueprint, detailing how the data is organized into hierarchical blocks called segments. Understanding WE30 allows you to visualize the data structure and identify where specific information resides within an IDoc.

4. SM59: RFC Destinations - The Connection "Phonebook"
Before data can travel, there needs to be a connection. SM59 (RFC Destinations) manages the technical "phone numbers" or connection parameters to other systems (whether other SAP systems or external applications). When an IDoc needs to be sent, it uses an RFC Destination (via a Port, typically defined in WE21) to establish the network link.

5. BD87: Status Monitor for ALE Messages - Your Troubleshooting Dashboard
This T-code is your best friend for monitoring and troubleshooting. BD87 allows you to see the status of all incoming and outgoing IDocs, drill down into errors, reprocess failed IDocs, and get a clear picture of what happened during an IDoc exchange. If an IDoc is stuck or failing, BD87 is almost always your first stop.

---

### The Bottom Line: Become an SAP Understander

In today's interconnected enterprise landscape, data exchange is paramount. Simply knowing how to use SAP modules isn't enough; true value comes from understanding how data flows, where it lives, and how to ensure its integrity across systems. Mastering IDocs will empower you to:

Communicate effectively with technical and functional teams.
Debug complex issues that span multiple systems.
Design robust solutions that consider end-to-end data flow.

So, don't just be an SAP user; be an SAP understander. Start exploring IDocs today, and watch your impact as an SAP professional grow!

What was your "aha!" moment with IDocs? Share your first troubleshooting story or your favorite IDoc T-code in the comments below!

 

https://www.linkedin.com/posts/mickaelquesnot_sapcareer-sapskills-idoctraining-activity-7333736111367741440-JZoR?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJPDL8BjpJe3thZB6t04k7Z2hRVHhboAec

---

entry.title: 📢 SAP Dummies Guide: Unlocking the Secrets of SAP IDoc Configuration! 🚀
entry.content:
SAP Dummies Guide: Unlocking the Secrets of SAP IDoc Configuration (Your Integration Cheat Sheet!)

Ever looked at complex SAP integration diagrams filled with transaction codes like WE20, BD64, SM59, WE21, WE30, WE81, WE42, and wondered how they all fit together? You're not alone! For many SAP beginners, the world of IDocs (Intermediate Documents) can seem daunting. But fear not, because this guide is your cheat sheet to understanding the backbone of SAP's powerful data exchange.

Understanding the IDoc configuration flow is absolutely crucial for anyone working with SAP integrations, whether you're a functional consultant, a developer, or a system administrator. This knowledge is what differentiates a basic user from an effective problem-solver in the SAP ecosystem.

The IDoc Communication Architecture: A High-Level View

Imagine two SAP systems (or an SAP system and an external application) needing to exchange business data – say, a Material Master record (MATMAS). They can't just "talk." They need a common language, a defined pathway, and a clear set of rules for what data goes where and what happens upon arrival. This is where IDoc configuration comes in.

The diagram you might have seen (and which we'll break down piece by piece) illustrates a typical setup for sending and receiving data. Let's demystify these interconnected components.

At the Heart of It All: The Partner Profile (WE20)

The Partner Profile (transaction code: WE20) is arguably the most critical piece of the IDoc puzzle. Think of it as your central address book and rulebook for all IDoc communication. It defines:

  • Who your SAP system talks to: These are your "partners" – other SAP clients, external systems (like a customer's or vendor's ERP), or even specific business units within your own company.
  • How your SAP system talks to them: It specifies the rules for sending data out (Outbound Parameters) and receiving data in (Inbound Parameters) for each specific Message Type.

Inside your Partner Profile (WE20), you'll configure:

  • Outbound Parameters: Here you define for a given partner:
    • Which Message Types (e.g., MATMAS, ORDERS) your system will send to them.
    • The Receiver Port (via WE21) through which the IDoc will physically leave your system.
    • The IDoc Type (via WE30) that defines the structure of the message.
    • Other settings like output mode (immediate or collected), packet size, etc.
  • Inbound Parameters: Here you define for a given partner:
    • Which Message Types your system expects to receive from them.
    • The Process Code (via WE42) that tells SAP what function module to execute when this IDoc arrives.
    • And other settings like error handling, syntax check, etc.

The Key Players in IDoc Communication (T-Codes Unveiled):

Let's break down the other important pieces that work in conjunction with WE20:

  1. Logical Systems (BD54 & SCC4):

    Every SAP client (e.g., your development client S18CLNT500, or your production client S18CLNT700) needs a unique "Logical System" name. This name uniquely identifies an individual system or client within your distributed landscape.

    • BD54 (Change View "Logical Systems"): This is where you define and list all the logical system names that participate in your ALE (Application Link Enabling) communication.
    • SCC4 (Client Settings): This transaction is crucial because it links the Logical System name to a specific SAP client. This tells SAP: "This client is identified by THIS logical system name."

  2. Message Types (WE81) & IDoc Types (WE30):

    These two work hand-in-hand to define the "what" and the "how" of your data.

    • WE81 (Message Type): This defines the business content of your message. For example, MATMAS signifies "Material Master Data," ORDERS means "Sales Order Data," and INVOIC represents "Invoice Data." It's the high-level description of what information is being exchanged.
    • WE30 (IDoc Type Development): This is the technical structure or blueprint of the IDoc. It defines how the data for a specific Message Type is organized internally. For instance, MATMAS06 is a specific version of an IDoc Type for Material Master.
    • WE82 (Message Type Assignment to IDoc Type): This T-code links the Message Type to the IDoc Type, telling SAP which technical structure to use for which business message. You can have multiple IDoc Types for one Message Type (e.g., for different versions).
    • WE31 (Segments): IDoc Types are built from smaller, reusable blocks called segments (e.g., E1MARAM for material general data, E1MARDM for plant data). Each segment holds specific pieces of data related to the overall business object. Understanding segments is vital for debugging!

  3. Model View (BD64):

    This is where you define the ALE (Application Link Enabling) Distribution Model. BD64 is your central cockpit for specifying which Message Types are exchanged between which Logical Systems. It's like drawing the communication lines on a map, specifying routes for specific types of data. It ensures that only relevant data is distributed to the correct receiving systems.

  4. Ports (WE21) & RFC Destinations (SM59):

    These define the technical pathway for the IDoc data to travel.

    • WE21 (Ports in IDoc Processing): A Port defines the technical characteristics of the sending or receiving channel. A common type for SAP-to-SAP communication is tRFC (Transactional RFC) Port, which ensures that the data package is sent reliably and once. For sending to external systems, you might use a File Port or an ABAP-RFC Port.
    • SM59 (Configuration of RFC Connections): This provides the actual "phone number" (connection details) to reach the other SAP system or external non-SAP application. The RFC Destination configured here is then linked to the Port (WE21). This is where you define host, port number, user, password, and network settings.

  5. Process Codes (WE42):

    For inbound IDocs, the Process Code (transaction code: WE42) is crucial. This code tells SAP what to do with the incoming IDoc data. It links a specific Message Type to a function module (e.g., IDOC_INPUT_MATMAS01 for material master) or a workflow that will process the IDoc's data and update your SAP system accordingly (e.g., create a material record, post an invoice, update a sales order).

Bringing It All Together

By meticulously setting up these interconnected pieces – Logical Systems, Partner Profiles with their Inbound/Outbound Parameters, Message and IDoc Types, Model Views, Ports, RFC Destinations, and Process Codes – your SAP system can seamlessly send and receive crucial business data with other systems. This automates processes, eliminates manual data entry, and ensures data consistency across your entire enterprise landscape.

Understanding these configuration steps isn't just about memorizing T-codes; it's about grasping the logic of data flow in complex SAP environments. This knowledge will empower you to not only set up new interfaces but also to confidently troubleshoot when things don't go as planned.

Do you work with IDocs in your daily role? What's your favorite transaction code from this list for troubleshooting or configuration? Share your insights and tips in the comments below!

 

https://www.linkedin.com/posts/mickaelquesnot_sap-idocs-sapintegration-activity-7336721588601446402-LhOT?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJPDL8BjpJe3thZB6t04k7Z2hRVHhboAec

#SAP #IDocs #SAPIntegration #ALE #SAPBasis #SAPDummies #MaterialMaster #DataExchange #SAPConfiguration #TechExplained #SAPCommunity #BeginnerSAP #SAPSkills

Our fiscal period runs from April to March. Currently, we are uploading the opening data for April.

While all other asset balances are correctly dated March 31, 2025, the AUC amount is being recorded with April 1, 2025. I also migrated the GL opening balance with the date 31.3.2025. When I checked the opening balance in the trial balance with the date 31.3.2025, the AUC amount did not appear, but when I generated it with 1.4.2025, it showed up. Hnin

entry.title: Announcing SAP Inside Track Kolkata – 2nd August 2025
entry.content:
We are thrilled to bring SAP Inside Track (SIT) Kolkata to life on August 2nd, 2025!

This is your exclusive opportunity to dive deep into the future of SAP Business AI across the full spectrum of SAP solutions, including SAP BTP, S/4HANA, Ariba, Concur, SuccessFactors, and more.

Join us in person as we explore how AI is revolutionizing SAP:

✅ Learn how to build AI solutions on SAP BTP
✅ Discover real-world stories from customers and partners using SAP Business AI
✅ Design intelligent use cases with Custom AI solutions
✅ Understand how SAP Build can power your business transformation
✅ Participate in hands-on sessions showcasing Embedded AI in SAP

🎯 Event Overview

SAP Inside Track Kolkata 2025 – Business AI Edition is designed for CXOs, Leaders, Developers, Consultants, Architects, and IT Managers or technical/business enthusiasts to gain practical insights into how AI integrates seamlessly with SAP products.

It’s your gateway to unlocking the true potential of SAP and Business AI within your organization.

SAP Inside Track (SIT) is a community-driven initiative where SAP professionals connect, share, and learn. Led by SAP Community members, it offers a space for real conversations, networking, and recognition in the SAP ecosystem.

We foster the culture of co-existence and collaboration; hence, we will have community members from the AWS Community & the Databricks Community. If you are already part of  another community and interested in collaborating, let’s connect!

entry.title: Re: Are premium AI features (require SAP AI Units) availa...
entry.content:
To enable and be able to demo the embedded AI capabilities, I believe partners need to acquire SAP AI Units (SKU 8018592) from the SAP customer (Not TDD) price list. This is the same process partners use to purchase SAP software for their own productive use. 

Partners should contact their SAP Account Executives (AE) to purchase AI units, or alternatively use this link to contact SAP sales for a quote. 

When the SAP AI Units have been added to the partner’s entitlements (this can be checked in “SAP for Me”, under the “Finance & Legal Business AI” tab), the partner can activate the individual AI features for each individual SAP embedded AI feature activation instructions.

A little more color commentary... I would love to be contradicted on this (and learn that a TDD license is available for direct purchase by a partner), but I think unfortunately today a sales rep needs to be involved. By way of one data point We (a partner and I) went into Manage my Services to try to add a TDD SKU for AI Units but we did not find it. So if you are having the same experience (or different) let us know. Thanks!

entry.title: Re: what is the retention period for the job logs in appl...
entry.content:
Hello,

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the retention period for application job logs and spool outputs is 30 days by default. After that, the system automatically deletes the data and cannot extend it.

To retain results longer, you must download or export them before expiry.

Best regards,
Chuma

entry.title: Extension ledger with type "S" on the top "P" extension ledger.
entry.content:
Dear all,

I want to create extension ledger with type "S" on the top "P" extension ledger.
However, when I try to create an extension ledger with "S" type using the "Define Extension Ledger" configuration,
I cannot find an extension ledger with the "P" type in the Underlying ledger of an extension ledger and cannot specify it.
(Refer to attached file.)
As shown below, the SAP documentation on multiple extension ledgers says it's possible, but I'd like to know if it's not possible in the public cloud or if I'm misunderstanding something.

On top of an extension ledger with the standard journal entries type, you can create an extension ledger of any type.
On top of a type P extension ledger, you can create another type P or type S extension ledger.
On top of a type S extension ledger, you can only create another type S extension ledger.

Thank you,

Su young.

entry.title: Re: Extension ledger with type "S" on the top "P" extensi...
entry.content:
Hello dear user,

And thank you for asking your question in the SAP Community blog.

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, your observation is correct: you cannot create an extension ledger of type "S" on top of an extension ledger of type "P" even though the SAP documentation mentions this as theoretically possible in general.

 

Key Clarification

The SAP documentation you're referring to most likely reflects SAP S/4HANA On-Premise or Private Cloud Edition behavior. In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (2SL), the extensibility and ledger configuration capabilities are more restricted by design to ensure system standardization and reduce complexity in upgrades and maintenance.

 

Your Issue in Detail:

You said:

"I cannot find an extension ledger with the 'P' type in the Underlying ledger of an extension ledger..."

This is expected in Public Cloud, where:

  • The "Underlying Ledger" field only shows the standard ledger (type 0) or base extension ledger (type P) if SAP allows it.
  • SAP does not currently allow creating a chain of extension ledgers (i.e. P → S) in the Public Cloud Edition even if technically possible in the Private Cloud or On-Premise environments.

 

Official Restriction

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition:

  • Allows creating one level of extension ledgers on top of the standard ledger.
  • Does not support chaining extension ledgers (P → S, P → P, S → S) in practice, even though documentation may suggest theoretical capability.

 

I hope this will help you answer your question.

Best regards,

Jeremy

entry.title: Re: Printing Adobe forms via local server or AWS
entry.content:
Hi @EmilioContreras 

You might have the bottleneck in the ABAP code or the ADS, which is Java. A little bit of information must be provided, in ABAP you can capture it with ST05 or SAT, I also like the STAT, if you are unfamiliar, ask your basis.

If it's on the ADS, tracing the Java is a little bit more tricky but it can be done, and ADS can be parallelized, look for note 2714231 and a java ADS parameter called "poolmax".

If the rest of your SAP servers are running on AWS, it does make sense to have the ADS running on AWS as well, yes

entry.title: KISS Your Complex AI Projects Goodbye
entry.content:
Another SAP Sapphire is in the books, and what a whirlwind of AI announcements it was! The annual conference wrapped up recently and was buzzing with excitement around artificial intelligence innovations across SAP's portfolio. From Joule's expanded capabilities to new partnerships with AI powerhouses, the message was clear: AI is transforming business, and SAP is at the forefront of this revolution.

The Rapid Evolution of Business AI

As I walked the show floor in Orlando, one thing became crystal clear – AI is no longer just a futuristic concept. It's here, it's evolving rapidly, and organizations are well-advised to think how this technology will transform their businesses. SAP's CEO Christian Klein emphasized this point during his keynote address, highlighting that we're "entering a new phase of AI with endless opportunities for all of us."

What struck me most was the sheer pace of innovation. The technology is advancing at breakneck speed (see a summary here), and businesses that don't start experimenting now risk being left behind.

"From humble beginnings come great things."

This ancient proverb perfectly captures where we stand with AI today. We're witnessing the early stages of a transformative technology that will reshape how businesses operate.

Our Journey with the SAP AppHaus

Amidst this AI revolution, our team at Syntax has been on our very own journey of exploration. Working closely with the SAP AppHaus team, we embarked on an adventure to understand how generative AI could solve real business challenges in a practical, approachable way.

Last year, this collaboration (read more) led to a significant milestone – the development of an AI-powered timesheet tracking.

This experience gave us valuable insights that we were thrilled to share during our presentation at Sapphire 2025, alongside Carlos Estala Velasco from the SAP AppHaus.

Simplicity by Design

For our presentation, we showcased our AI-powered time-tracking solution. In comparison to all the AI-centered announcements and demos during the keynotes, our solution may look a bit simple, and some people may cannot help it, but be reminded of Shania Twain's song: "That don't impress me much!"

At first glance, our demonstration might have seemed underwhelming – a simple use case (just a time-sheet entry solution), straightforward architecture, and a clean, minimalist interface (typical chatbot UX)

But that simplicity? It was entirely by design.

We deliberately embraced the KISS principle (Keep It Simple, Stupid) for several important reasons:

  1. Learning Focus: By keeping the business case straightforward, we could focus on learning the fundamentals of building AI solutions without getting bogged down in complex requirements.
  2. Clear Value Demonstration: The simplicity allowed us to clearly demonstrate the value of AI in a relatable context that everyone could understand. And our consultants love it, as it greatly speeds up this dull, repetitive process.
  3. Faster Iteration: A simpler solution meant we could iterate quickly, learning and adapting as we went along.
  4. Accessibility: The approachable nature of the project made it easier for team members with varying technical backgrounds to contribute and learn.

As Leonardo de Araujo, our SVP of Global SAP Innovation, noted during the presentation:
"As early adopters of SAP S/4HANA Cloud and participants in the GenAI Lighthouse program, we've used SAP Business AI to tackle industry challenges like inefficient timesheet processing. This innovation has the potential of significantly reducing our Days Sales Outstanding. Our estimates show we can save up to $100,000 per day of DSO reduction by shortening the billing cycle."

Key Takeaways: Start Small, Think Big

If there's one message we wanted attendees to take home from our presentation, it's this: don't wait for the perfect, complex use case to start your AI journey. Instead:

  1. Start Small: Begin with simple, focused use cases where AI can deliver clear value.
  2. Use Proper Methodology: Leverage structured approaches like those offered by the SAP AppHaus to identify and develop meaningful use cases.
  3. Foster an AI Culture: Encourage experimentation and learning throughout your organization.
  4. Look Beyond ERP: Many valuable AI use cases extend beyond traditional ERP boundaries.
  5. Embrace the Platform: SAP is building a robust AI foundation – leverage it as it continues to evolve.

The Path Forward

As we reflect on our AI journey so far, we're more convinced than ever that AI represents a transformative opportunity for businesses. But realizing this potential doesn't require massive, complex projects from the start. In fact, the opposite is often true – beginning with simple, focused applications allows organizations to build competence, confidence, and a foundation for more ambitious initiatives. As we did with progressing from our humble beginnings to launching our first SAP-certified GenAI offering: our Syntax AI CodeGenie solution, which helps SAP customers to document their custom code and interact with this documentation (e.g. to prepare for their S/4 migration.)

The SAP AppHaus offers an excellent starting point with their Business AI Explore Workshop, which helps identify meaningful use case opportunities, and their Business AI Design Workshop, which transforms ideas into feasible solution concepts.

In the words of Joyce Meyer, "Small beginnings are the launching pad to great endings."

Our simple timesheet tracking application may not have been the most impressive demo at Sapphire, but it represents something more important – a practical first step on what promises to be an extraordinary journey. As such, let me encourage all of you to take that first step and start exploring AI. Or even better, share your own story about the journey so far! I'd be happy to hear how you tackled it!

entry.title: how to add standard field min. shelf life expiration date in MM41/MM42/MM43
entry.content:
Hello SAP Experts,

I'm working with SAP Retail and trying to ensure that the standard field "Minimum Shelf Life Expiration Date" (e.g., MARC-MHDRZ) is not visible in the material master transactions MM41 (Create Article) and MM42 (Change Article). Screen sequence is already maintained and this is a standard field (not a custom one).

What I’ve Tried:
- Checked field selection groups in `OMS9`
- Verified Field Reference in `OMS2`
- The field is not hidden in configuration

Question:
How can I make this field appear in both MM41 and MM42?
Is this purely configuration-related or is screen enhancement (SE51 or BADI) required?

Any guidance or steps would be highly appreciated.

entry.title: Re: Is there a way to reprice PO items in mass?
entry.content:
Hello @Yousssef123698745 

Yes, that’s correct. In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, PO repricing is only possible per item, and only if no follow-on documents exist (like goods receipts or invoices).

There is currently no standard option to reprice POs in bulk, and no extensibility workaround due to cloud restrictions.

check this SAP Help Portal for Manage Purchase Orders.

With kind regards

Chuma
